verses.commentary: Still fearful despite wrestling with God.
Servants first. Leah next.
Jacob bows seven times. Reversal of the stolen blessing where nations would bow to him (27:29).
Like the prodigal son's father (Luke 15:20).

Jacob answered, They are the children God has been gracious enough to give your servant. Who are these? The children God has been gracious to give. Jacob attributes his family to divine grace. Your servant.
Servants approach. Bowed down.
Last of all came Joseph and Rachel, and they too bowed down.
Esau questions the lavish gifts. Jacob's strategy is working.
I have plenty. My brother. Esau is generous.
To see your face is like seeing the face of God. After seeing God's face at Peniel (32:30), his brother's forgiveness reflects divine grace.
God has been gracious. Jacob recognizes divine blessing.
Let's go. I'll accompany you.
Children are tender, livestock are nursing. Must move slowly. Practical wisdom and care.
Avoiding commitment?.
Jacob politely declines the escort. Just let me find favor. Some wariness remains, or perhaps he wants independence to move at his own pace.
Esau returns to Seir. Jacob goes to Sukkoth. They part peacefully but separately.
Made shelters (sukkot, סֻכּוֹת) for livestock.
At Shechem. God's promise fulfilled (28:15): brought back safely.
Like Abraham buying burial ground (chapter 23).
Set up an altar. The altar proclaims: El Elohe Israel, God, the God of Israel. Identity and worship converge.
